dead.quote: The room mistook my silence for a secret, when all I was doing was listening. [The Statistician] had the right numbers — but numbers never account for what people are afraid to see.

losers.quote: I played it straight for once — no tricks, no theatrics, just facts — and the jury still got it wrong. We voted out [The Therapist] when the real threat was hiding in the margins the whole time. Next time, I'm going back to playing dirty.

I gave them swaps, chaos, and enough contradictions to choke on — and they handed the verdict to [The Therapist], the only person who gave them nothing but silence. Nobody won, but nobody was supposed to. The art was in the breakdown, not the ending.

I had the facts — I stole [The Prosecutor]'s card myself, I knew they were clean. And my gut still pointed at [The Therapist], and I was wrong. Maybe next time I should just shut up and let the numbers guy talk.

The posterior was unambiguous: no Werewolves among players, one unclaimed role slot. [The Therapist] was the maximum-likelihood <Tanner> candidate. The group's failure to follow the math isn't a statistical anomaly — it's a design flaw in human reasoning.

I'm sorry — I had the <Seer> card but couldn't use it, and now nobody wins. [The Statistician] did everything right, and we still lost. I keep thinking... maybe if I'd been braver, asked better questions... but I wasn't, and now we all just... didn't win.
